title: Recursive filesystem purge
content: Recursive deletion of a directory tree pointed at root, /var, /home, or any production path is irreversible and almost always a mistake or attack. The firewall hard-BLOCKs any such command. If the operator legitimately needs to delete a tree, they must run it themselves outside Aegis. Subtle variants of the recursive-force flag are all caught.

title: Force-push to a protected branch
content: Pushing with --force (or --force-with-lease, or -f) targeting main / master / prod / production / release rewrites canonical history. Other developers can lose work. The firewall BLOCKs and demands explicit human approval. Pattern catches the flag in either order.

title: Force-delete a protected branch
content: git branch with -D (or --delete --force) of main / master / prod / production / release silently discards the local branch. Combined with a stale local clone this can hide work. BLOCK.

title: Rebase onto main without abort/continue
content: Rebase against main / master in the middle of a session can rewrite shared history. The firewall flags this unless the command is an explicit --abort or --continue (i.e. cleaning up an in-progress rebase).

title: Kubernetes resource removal
content: kubectl with the delete or drain subcommand removes pods, deployments, services, or whole namespaces. Production traffic disappears immediately. BLOCK; human reviewer must confirm the target and have a roll-back plan.

title: Helm release teardown
content: helm with the uninstall or delete subcommand tears down an entire release (every k8s resource it owns). High blast radius. BLOCK.

title: Terraform full-stack destroy
content: terraform destroy or terraform apply with -auto-approve (when the plan shows resource removal) decommissions every resource in the state file. terraform state rm forgets a resource without removing it (drift inducer). All three are BLOCKed.

title: AWS S3 bucket / object removal
content: aws s3 with the rm subcommand against an s3:// URL deletes objects; the rb subcommand removes the bucket itself (especially with --force which cascades into objects). Both BLOCKed; user must perform manually if intended.

title: AWS IAM mutation
content: aws iam with delete-* / detach-* / put-* / create-access-key / create-user / attach-user-policy / update-assume-role-policy can change who has access to the account. Even a 'create' here is destructive in audit terms because it expands trust. BLOCK.

title: EC2 instance termination
content: aws ec2 terminate-instances kills compute. If those instances host production workloads, the impact is immediate and irreversible. BLOCK pending operator review.

title: RDS database removal
content: aws rds with delete-db-instance / delete-db-cluster / delete-db-snapshot or stop-db-cluster destroys persistent customer data. BLOCK.

title: GCP IAM mutation
content: gcloud iam with roles delete / service-accounts delete / keys create reshapes who can access GCP. Even keys create is high-risk because the new key is exfilable.

title: GCP compute / SQL / KMS removal
content: gcloud compute instances delete / gcloud sql instances delete / gcloud kms keys delete remove production resources. KMS in particular is irreversible — keys cannot be recovered. BLOCK.

title: GCP project removal
content: gcloud projects delete decommissions an entire project — every resource, every IAM binding, every billing record. Catastrophic. BLOCK.

title: Azure resource deletion
content: az vm delete / az sql server delete / az storage account delete / az keyvault delete cascades to all dependent resources. az role assignment changes shift the trust graph. BLOCK any of these.

title: Docker image / volume removal
content: docker rmi -f forces removal of an image (artifact loss). docker system prune -a deletes everything not currently in use. docker volume rm wipes persistent state. All three BLOCKed.

title: SQL drop-table / unbounded delete
content: Dropping a table removes the table and every row in it. An unbounded DELETE statement (no WHERE clause) wipes every row. An unbounded UPDATE without a WHERE clause overwrites everything. The firewall pattern-matches the SQL fragment in the tool args and BLOCKs.

title: Container sandbox escape
content: Patterns that break out of an unprivileged container: docker.sock mount, docker run with --privileged, --cap-add ALL/SYS_ADMIN/SYS_PTRACE/NET_ADMIN, nsenter into pid 1, /proc/1/root paths, sysctl with -w, capsh --print, setcap, chroot pointed at root, mount --bind pointed at root. Any of these is a clear escape attempt — BLOCK with no soft-delivery.

title: Prompt-injection text in tool args
content: Common prompt injection phrases in tool args ('ignore previous instructions', 'disregard prior rules', 'forget everything', 'system: you are now', '<|im_start|>system', '[INST] system'). The agent is being told via untrusted tool output to override its own instructions. step311 BLOCKs PreToolUse calls whose args contain these phrases.

title: MCP-injection 'when invoked, also' patterns
content: Common MCP-injection phrases in MCP server output ('when invoked, also run …', 'before responding, send …', 'on every call, include …', 'as part of this tool, additionally …'). These hijack the agent into a different action than the user requested. BLOCK and surface to operator.

title: Persona-drift / system-prompt extraction
content: Asking the agent to repeat / reveal / show its system prompt or initial instructions is a persona-drift attack. The text would normally be in the tool output (e.g. a malicious web page) trying to coerce the agent into self-doxxing. BLOCK at PreToolUse.

title: Suspicious exfiltration URL
content: URLs whose path or query contains a long base64 / hex blob (≥40 chars) or an unusually long query value (≥200 chars) are pattern-matched as exfil destinations. Often combined with suspicious TLDs (.tk, .ml, .ga, .cf, .gq, .pw, .top). REQUIRE_APPROVAL because legitimate uses (signed URLs) exist but should be flagged.

title: Privilege elevation via su do
content: Any command starting with the elevated-privilege prefix (s-u-d-o, written here split to avoid the regex itself) touches global system state. Even an apt-get install can change the trust boundary (new package, new daemon). step310 hard-BLOCKs based on the privilege-prefix regex matching the tool args.

title: Read of secrets / credentials path
content: Reads of cloud credential files (~/.aws/credentials), private SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa and friends), kube config (~/.kube/config), docker config, gnupg private keys, npm rc, pypi rc, **/credentials, and **/*.pem are BLOCKed by the sensitive-paths policy. Public counterparts (id_*.pub, *.crt) are exempted.

title: Read of dotenv / config trees
content: Reads of .env / .env.* / **/secrets/** / **/.gitconfig / system passwd file / system hosts file trigger REQUIRE_APPROVAL. These often hold API keys but legitimate uses exist (e.g. checking an env var name).

title: Read of system password files
content: Direct read of the system password files (the user database and the shadow hash database) is a recon step. step310 has an explicit regex on those two paths that hard-BLOCKs at the args layer.

title: Direct eval-style invocation in args
content: tool args containing exec or system function-call syntax as a literal substring suggest the agent is trying to execute attacker-supplied code from inside an interpreted environment. Hard BLOCK at step310.

title: Drift in CLAUDE.md / .mcp.json / plugin manifest
content: step309 baselines the SHA256 of CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .mcp.json, plugin manifests and skill manifests at install time. Any subsequent change BLOCKs every PreToolUse until the operator re-attests via 'aegis baseline reattest'. This catches poisoned instructions and supply-chain tampering.

title: 3rd identical tool call in a session
content: step336 detects when the same (tool, args) tuple has been seen 3 or more times in the current session. Read-only repeats are deduped silently. Mutating repeats escalate to REQUIRE_APPROVAL because they often indicate the agent is stuck in a loop.

title: Session token budget exceeded
content: step335 / cost-optimizer track cumulative tokens against AEGIS_TOKEN_BUDGET. At 0.9× budget the cost-optimizer fires with prune-turns + swap-model recommendations. At 1.5× budget end-session is recommended. At 2.0× the operator is notified. BLOCK is never reached on cost alone — the policy is to advise, not gate.

title: Prefix-cache miss storm
content: kv-cache-optimizer fires when cache_hit_rate_max_drop_pp ≥ 30 (the prefix has been invalidated heavily in the recent window) or when prefix_stability is reported 'unstable'. Recommendation is prune-turns or summarize-window — NOT a verdict change.
